topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The morning mail ritual usually brought a mild sense of anticipation, a small, predictable joy in the rustle of envelopes. But today, as I lifted the flap of the topmost letter, my fingers froze halfway. Tucked beneath a utility bill was a card. It was thick, cream-colored cardstock, entirely blank save for a single, elegant script on the front: "Happy Birthday." There was no name, no address, just the sentiment floating in the crisp air. I held it up to the light, turning it over, expecting a hidden message, a signature, anything to anchor it to reality. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my throat. I set it down gently on the counter, my gaze darting around the quiet living room as if searching for an explanation etched into the wallpaper. Who would send this? And why, exactly,?

[story 2]

I picked up the card with the back of my hand, the paper feeling strangely smooth, almost cool. It was an unsigned birthday greeting. My brow furrowed deeply, the skin tightening across my forehead. I turned it over again, scrutinizing the edges, searching for any watermark or faint indentation that might reveal a sender. Silence hung in the apartment, amplifying the slight sound of my own restless shifting in my chair. I ran a fingertip over the plain surface, a slow, deliberate motion. It felt like holding a secret. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest, a kind of cognitive dissonance. It wasn't wrong, just… out of place. I placed the card back down, my jaw working silently as I tried to reconcile the simple message with the sheer absence of any context.

[story 3]

The mail slot usually delivered predictable junk—ads, flyers, bills. This time, however, there it was: a card. Unaddressed. It had been slipped in with the usual assortment of paper, yet it stood out, an anomaly in the mundane. I picked it up, my hands trembling slightly as I examined the inscription. "Happy Birthday." It was undeniably a birthday wish, yet it lacked the necessary components—no name, no return address. I stared at the card for a long moment, my eyes unfocused, trying to pull meaning from the stark emptiness. A low hum of confusion vibrated behind my eyes. I turned the card over and over, my movements becoming jerky, a sign that my mind was struggling to process the sheer illogicality of the situation.
